speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four with Ben Gerstein. OK, so you're about to get on the boat then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I-- I did get on the boat. And the rest of my family-- my-- my mother, my father, and I. He says that we-- we came from Fohrenwald. We went to Bremenhaven to wait to get on the boat. But as I just mentioned before, that they chose only one of our family. So they chose me. My father chose me, my mother. And I went ahead. About a month and a half or two months later, the second boat came in to pick up the rest. And I was waiting. I came over to the harbor with my aunt from New York to meet my-- my family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember seeing a crowd of people. And I see my sister running to me, and my mother. And I kissed them. We hugged. And I said, where's my father? Where's Dad? Were is Father? And-- and I see them broke down, and start crying. And I said, what happened? Why? You know, I was in shock.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a stroke, and then two days later he died in Bremen. It was 1948. Going through the war, going through all that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 48 years old. A young man. And-- and all of a sudden, I had to grow up fast. Overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You became the man of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became the man of the family. Yeah. I worked at a job in New York-- as a dental technician, as a matter of fact-- for about a month and a half, until my uncle from Toronto-- that was on my mother's side, my mother's brother-- came to New York and packed us into his Chrysler car, and took us into Toronto with whatever belongings we had. And he brought us into Toronto. And I've been in Toronto ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in early December, first week in December, I believe, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to leave New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know the difference. I mean, I knew New York very little, and Canada very little. My uncle brought me here, so I'm here. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work in Toronto when you first arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked when I first arrived, yes. I got again in a dental lab. I got a job as a dental technician. And after that, I did a lot of different things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't like being a dental technician?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't like being a dental technician. And-- and North America is different than Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what sense? In here, everything is-- is production. And naturally, you cannot produce as well if you do everything. But if you specialize in one item, you do it well. And that's how they do-- do it in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: One guy does the crowns. One guy does in the upper or the lower plates. One guy does the-- the bridging. You see what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they put me doing one thing every day, I said, wait a minute, this is a factory. And I used to work for a dentist. We-- and In Europe, mostly that every dentist had his own technician, his own lab. So I was like part of his crew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, here they put you behind that little desk, which is probably as big as maybe a little wider than my chair-- about yea wide. And that's where I said, is that where I'm going to spend the rest of my life? And he says, what's the matter with you? You got a job. I said, uh-uh, that's not for me. And my father wasn't there to, you see, to influence me. So I worked there for two and have weeks, and I quit. I didn't know how to stay quit in English. [CHUCKLES] He phoned my mother. He was complaining at all that, you know. I don't know whether he really is a young boy. He shouldn't make a mistake. Look at the people going around and don't have jobs. She said, well what can I do? He doesn't like it. From then on, I kicked around for a while. And I did a lot of different things. I finally settled down into furniture retail eventually, and then into real estate. So what was your-- you just arrived in a new country. Did you have any dreams? Did you still want to be a race car driver?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I forgot all about it. Forgot all about it. Too busy trying to survive, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were your new dreams then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What were my dreams? Appreciating life. My dream was to realize that life is so short, and how important it is to appreciate that you're living. But unless you're going to have a close brush with something that threatens your life, it's hard to appreciate. You know, we take it for granted, that we are all alive, we're living. Do nothing. You can appreciate life, do nothing. It doesn't have to-- you don't have to be busy 24 hours a day. It doesn't mean that you have to travel the world a hundred times over, and try everything. Just appreciate that you're alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1970-- August 16th, 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rita, maiden name Gottlieb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children. My daughter, 24 years old. My son, Josh. My girl's name is Francine. My son Josh, 21 years old. And my son David, which is just about 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's going for medicine. She is-- she's into her-- going into her third year of medicine. My Josh is going for law and MBA combined. My little guy's going for something, I hope. [CHUCKLES] He's just going through school. He's not sure yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all live in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all live in Toronto. They all live with us. That used to go to University of Western Ontario. They went through that routine. And my daughter is now going to Toronto General Hospital. And she's going to be a third year med, as I said-- teaching hospital. My son is U of T. And my young son doesn't know what-- where he's going to go now [? that ?] this coming year yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do for a living right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I'm still involved in-- in holdings, in real estate holdings. I'm running operation. Almost semi-retired, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And who of your family lives here in Toronto now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousins, and stuff like that, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just people that you've mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister lives here. My sister lives here with her husband. And she's got a son. That's my immediate-- my sister. My mother just passed away in-- in February. I have a cousin. I have a cousin living in New York. And I have two cousins living-- three cousins living in New York. And I have a couple of cousins living in Toronto. And then that-- nothing that close, other than my immediate-- my sister and my mom. And that's it, for the moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you think your-- your experiences changed the way you were brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try to make him aware of what happened. And it's hard to do that. It's hard to make them comprehend. And yet I remember telling the story to my aunt when we first came to Toronto. And I remember her answer was, I know you're not lying. I know that. I read in the paper. But I don't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, I cannot comprehend that that-- that this is humanity, this is-- this is what's happened. She said, I cannot make self to realistically say, yeah, I know what happened. I can picture that's what happened. She said, I couldn't do it. She couldn't do it. So how do we get kids to really understand that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope to go with them to Poland to the-- to the-- to go through Israel, through the Holocaust and all of that, you know, as much as you can. But at the same time, I want them to know what happened. I wanted to forget what happened. I want to forget what happened. You can't. For sure, I can't forget. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. You try to live. We try to live. And I have a lot of gentile friends, good friends, and I get along. I've always felt that way. But you can't forget that you had no place to run. If you found one righteous one, that was like a miracle. So that's the memory you have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to leave a message to your future grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Don't forget your heritage. Don't forget the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] If nothing for them, it's for [SOB] for the 6 million people. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Just-- just as a-- like a headstone on a grave-- just the memory. Very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much for sharing your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I'm introducing you here to my lovely wife Rita, my 21-year-old boy Josh, and my almost 20-year-old boy David. Missing here, my 24-year-old daughter, Francine. And this is my immediate family. My last words, my last message is I hope they grow up as survivors, as kids of a surviving father, mother. And I just hope they never forget-- that's-- that's my goal in life, somehow to relate to them not to forget what happened. They don't have to live with it, have nightmares about it, but not to forget. And their children and grandchildren should not forget. Thank you. These are photographs of a portrait of my grandmother and my grandfather. My grandfather's name is Jacob. And my grandmother's name is [? Passa, ?] I believe. They were also lived-- that I remember-- most of their lives in-- in the same area as we were brought up in Poland. And they were liquidated also in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandfather's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shostack. Now this picture is of my mother, myself, and my sister. My-- my sister's on the right. She's the taller one. And that's me on the left. I'm about four years old. My sister is seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell us the name of everyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ben, Musia, and Beila Gerstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Shostack.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell us your mother's full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beila Gerstein, her married name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your sister's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister's full name is Musia, that's how she was called. And my full name is Ben-- or back then, I was born Benzion Gerstein. And here, roughly, I was about 7 years old, my sister was 10. We were-- that was our main street in Luck. We were on the way to school, which was very close by.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm on the right, of course, my sister's on my left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Musia. And my name is Ben. Here, we have my mother, and my sister. My mother Beila, my sister, Musia. And they were just coming from a festival. It's a typical Polish festival they had every year commemorating whatever it was. And it's a typical Polish uniform that my sister is-- is wearing. Fall-- fall dance. She was coming from a dance. She was, I believe, 12 years old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the fate of your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my sister's still living here. She's in Toronto with her husband and son. And my mother passed away at 88, at the Baycrest in February of '95. This is the family that we've spent the last nine months until the liberation. That's where we hid out. And naturally, thanks to them, they were the righteous ones. They saved our lives-- all of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the family is the Capek. That's a typical Czech name, Capek. He is shown there with his wife, on his left, and three daughters in the background, and a small boy. At that time, he was around eight years old. He never knew through the whole ordeal that we were hiding on his-- in his farm. The minute they were-- the minute we got liberated, two months later, he got drafted into the army, Mr. Capek did. And he saw killings and everything, that he was never used to it. He was a very gentle farmer. And he wrote us a few letters about what was-- what he saw. And then we heard that he got injured. Well, I met this Czechoslovakian general, an army general when we first got liberated. He took a liking to me. And I introduced him to my father. And my father and I, we were begging him somehow to save this man's life. Well, in a [? bad ?] way we did, because he never went back to the army. He got operated, basically after the war, it was finished. And he was at my sister's wedding. And all he did the whole night was kiss my father, kiss us, cry, and dance. I'll never forget that. And he says, my-- I still believe in my God. And you believe in your God. And we are both good people. And that's the speech he gave at the wedding. He couldn't finish it. He kept crying. [INAUDIBLE] He was not with us, don't forget. Here we have at the back, left to right, my cousin Bruce-- Bruce Gerstein-- about my age. Next to him, my father Shia. Myself, at the back. My name is Ben. In front of me, my sister, Musia. To her right my mother, Beila-- Gerstein, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Fohrenwald, Germany in 1946. That's where we stayed for almost three years to be emigrated to, as it happened, to the United States from there. Here, we have-- this is also in Fohrenwald, by the way, around 1946, maybe '47. That's at the back of the townhouse that we stayed in. Left to right in the background is my-- my uncle-- I don't recall his name, my father in the middle-- Shia-- myself, Ben. In front of me again, Musia. Next to her right is my mother. And that's my aunt to the right of my mother. And I don't remember her name. And that's her little boy that I see there in front. Of course, I don't remember his name. It was around 1947 in Fohrenwald, Germany. This picture was taken-- I seen an address on it-- in-- in 1958 on Borden Street. That's where my aunt lived, in her living room in the background. Left to right my stepfather, Shlomo Grynspan. Next to him is Alex Schneiderman, my uncle. Next to him is Irving Greenberg, my cousin. Myself, in the-- in the background, Ben. In front of me is my cousin, Florence Greenberg. To her right is her mother-- my aunt-- Shostack, [? Hyka ?] Shostack. To her right is my mother, Beila. And to her right is her sister Jenny.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And in which city is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This in Toronto, Borden Street is in Toronto-- downtown. Ah, my wedding day, wedding photograph. My lovely wife, Rita Miriam Gottlieb, her middle name. And that's me, Ben Gerstein. We were married in August 16th, 1970 in Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue were you married in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beth Am.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beth Am, on Jane Street-- on Keele Street. I remember that [INAUDIBLE]. Here, we have my family photograph of my son's-- Joshua Gerstein's-- bar mitzvah. It was June 1986, at Adath Israel shul. And that's in Toronto. In the background, left to right, my son David, my daughter, Francine, the bar mitzvah boy, Josh. In front of him, myself, Ben, and my wife, Rita Gerstein. This is a picture of our family taken in 1992 in-- in Toronto. And my wife on the front left side-- Rita; myself, Ben. In the background-- my son, Josh, and my younger son, David, and my daughter, which is the oldest, Francine Gerstein.
